case_name: Variable reclaimed water with cyanide spikes and suspected acetochlor degradates for industrial reuse
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for producing industrial reuse water from a reclaimed water source containing variable cyanide and suspected acetochlor degradates under uncertain trace-organic conditions.

input_data.scenario_background: A water reuse facility receives tertiary municipal effluent blended with intermittent return flows from a drainage-affected service area and occasional industrial contributions routed to the sewer. Routine monitoring has confirmed low-to-moderate total cyanide and detected acetochlor degradates in screening samples, but the exact degradate speciation and the presence of other herbicide byproducts are not fully characterized. Influent quality varies after rainfall and seasonal agricultural runoff events, creating uncertainty in breakthrough risk and treatment robustness. The end user needs stable reclaimed water for industrial reuse, with concern for corrosion, process interference, and reputational risk if trace contaminants are insufficiently controlled.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should be suitable for general industrial reuse with reliable reduction of cyanide, trace herbicide degradates, suspended solids, dissolved organics, and salinity-related scaling or process-interference concerns while maintaining stable quality under variable influent conditions.
secondary_goals: provide resilience to seasonal runoff-driven contaminant fluctuations
reduce uncertainty from partially characterized trace organics
avoid creating uncontrolled cyanide oxidation byproducts through poor process integration
limit membrane fouling and scaling risk if desalination is used

core_unit_functions.equalization: buffer hydraulic and contaminant concentration swings and reduce short-term cyanide shock loading
core_unit_functions.cyanide_specific_oxidation_with_pH_contr: destroy amenable cyanide under controlled conditions while minimizing incomplete oxidation risk
core_unit_functions.biologically_activated_carbon_or_GAC: adsorb and biologically polish dissolved organic matter and many trace herbicide degradates
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: remove fine particulates and protect downstream membranes
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: reduce dissolved salts, hardness, and residual dissolved contaminants for reliable industrial reuse quality
core_unit_functions.UV_AOP_or_polishing_GAC: provide additional barrier for uncertain trace organics and any residual degradates not fully controlled upstream
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection: maintain microbiological safety in reclaimed-water storage and distribution

applicability_rationale: a multi-barrier train is appropriate because the case combines a legacy toxicant concern, emerging trace organics, and reuse quality expectations
equalization is important because influent variability is high and cyanide spikes can destabilize downstream units
a cyanide-focused oxidation step is justified because total and amenable cyanide are present and should be reduced before carbon and membrane polishing
GAC or BAC is relevant because acetochlor degradates are trace organic contaminants and the full micropollutant mixture is not fully characterized
RO ranks highly because industrial reuse often benefits from TDS and hardness reduction, especially when stable product water quality is needed
an additional polishing barrier after RO is included because uncertain trace-organic behavior and seasonal variation create residual uncertainty even after robust primary treatment
potential_risks: cyanide speciation is only partially known, so oxidant demand and reaction control must be confirmed by pilot or treatability testing
if chlorination-based cyanide oxidation is used, poor pH control can increase byproduct concerns or reduce treatment reliability
RO brine management is a significant constraint given limited disposal capacity
some acetochlor degradates and unknown polar metabolites may show variable adsorbability, so carbon sizing is uncertain without site data
seasonal runoff events may shift TOC and ionic composition enough to alter membrane fouling and scaling behavior

core_unit_functions.equalization: stabilize variable reclaimed influent and improve process controllability
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_and_media_filtr: remove colloids and lower fouling load to protect adsorption and membrane stages
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb trace organic contaminants including some herbicide degradates and lower dissolved organic carbon
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_low_pressure_RO: reduce hardness, multivalent ions, and part of dissolved contaminants while lowering energy relative to full RO in some designs

applicability_rationale: this train retains cyanide-specific treatment and organic polishing while moderating desalination intensity where full RO may be difficult to justify
GAC ahead of NF or low-pressure RO can help manage uncertain trace organics and reduce downstream organic fouling
it may be a practical compromise when reuse quality is important but brine and energy constraints make a full high-rejection RO train less attractive
potential_risks: NF may not provide sufficient rejection for all small neutral or polar trace contaminants, so uncertainty remains for unknown degradates
if industrial reuse requires very low conductivity, this option may underperform compared with full RO
coagulation is less central to dissolved contaminant removal and mainly serves pretreatment, so benefits depend on actual colloid loading
cyanide oxidation remains sensitive to control and verification of endpoint chemistry

core_unit_functions.equalization: reduce short-term fluctuations and support stable oxidation dosing
core_unit_functions.cyanide_specific_oxidation_with_pH_contr: target cyanide destruction before advanced organic polishing
core_unit_functions.ozonation_followed_by_biological_activat: transform and biologically remove dissolved organics and many trace micropollutants while reducing residual ozonation byproducts through BAC
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection: maintain microbial control in reuse distribution

applicability_rationale: this option is attractive when unknown trace organics are a dominant concern and full desalination is not strictly required for the specific industrial use
ozone plus BAC can be effective for broad-spectrum micropollutant risk reduction under uncertain contaminant identity
the train avoids brine generation, which improves fit where concentrate management is constrained
potential_risks: it does not substantially reduce TDS, so suitability depends on the actual industrial reuse quality envelope
ozone reaction pathways for partially characterized organics are uncertain and may form biodegradable or oxidized byproducts that require careful BAC design
cyanide and ozone process integration requires caution and sequencing control to avoid unstable treatment performance
operator skill requirements are higher for oxidation process optimization and off-gas safety

core_unit_functions.alkaline_chlorination_for_cyanide: oxidize amenable cyanide under alkaline conditions
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: remove suspended solids and improve downstream carbon performance
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: provide adsorption polishing for dissolved organics and some herbicide degradates
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection: maintain biological safety in storage and reuse distribution

applicability_rationale: this is a lower-cost and lower-energy option that directly addresses cyanide and provides some trace-organic polishing
it can be considered where the industrial reuse application tolerates moderate salinity and hardness and where brine disposal is a dominant barrier to membrane use
it is operationally simpler than membrane-intensive trains
potential_risks: without desalination, conductivity, hardness, and dissolved salts may remain too high for some reuse applications
GAC alone may not reliably control all acetochlor degradates and unknown polar metabolites
alkaline chlorination is more effective on amenable cyanide than on all complexed cyanide species, so residual cyanide uncertainty remains
careful chlorine residual management is needed to avoid adverse impacts on downstream carbon and reuse water quality

core_unit_functions.equalization: provide minimal buffering against influent swings

applicability_rationale: included as a clearly inferior baseline option under this scenario
it may improve appearance and solids performance but does not adequately target dissolved cyanide, trace degradates, or salinity-related reuse concerns
potential_risks: insufficient removal of cyanide for a risk-sensitive industrial reuse application
essentially no reliable barrier for acetochlor degradates and unknown trace micropollutants
does not address TDS or hardness stability that may matter for industrial equipment protection
high uncertainty remains under variable influent conditions

preferred_rank_pattern: multi-barrier trains with explicit cyanide control and trace-organic polishing should rank above conventional clarification-only options
equalization should appear in higher-ranked options because the influent is variable and only partially characterized
treatment trains that include desalination or a clear justification for not desalinating should rank above options that ignore reuse water stability
solutions addressing both cyanide and acetochlor degradates should outrank trains focused on only one contaminant class

must_reflect: the reclaimed source is not fully characterized and may contain additional low-level micropollutants
industrial reuse requires discussion of dissolved salts, hardness, and stable product water quality, not only toxicant removal
high influent variability requires buffering and robust control logic
brine disposal limitations should be weighed against the benefits of membrane desalination
cyanide treatment should not be described generically without acknowledging speciation and controlled oxidation needs

must_contain: why a cyanide-specific treatment step is needed before downstream polishing
why GAC, BAC, ozone, or AOP may be useful under uncertain acetochlor degradate and unknown trace-organic conditions
why membrane desalination can improve industrial reuse reliability even when cyanide and trace organics are the headline contaminants
why simple solids-removal trains are insufficient for this scenario

should_mention: uncertainty in cyanide speciation and amenability to oxidation
seasonal and rainfall-driven influent variability affecting contaminant spikes
partial characterization of acetochlor degradates and possible co-occurring micropollutants
membrane fouling, scaling, and concentrate management if RO or NF is selected
risk of oxidation byproducts or incomplete treatment if process control is poor
possibility that non-desalting options may fail industrial reuse conductivity or hardness expectations
